1:
On Numbers 15:1 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the gracious laws given after the rebellion of the spies.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: after the unhappy attempt to enter Canaan against God’s will, Israel remained many days in Kadesh, and these laws follow. The Puritan Henry: the law begins very encouragingly, “when ye come into the land.” The Reformed Gill: spoken after the murmurings over the spies and the sentence in the wilderness. Calvin: he partly repeats and partly gathers the precepts of Leviticus.

The verse opens new laws. After the great rebellion and its sentence, God speaks fresh statutes for the land. Grace follows hard upon judgment.

So the witnesses see God, just after the terrible sentence on the rebellious generation, speak laws that take for granted Israel will yet possess the land. The witnesses note the striking placing of these statutes: Gill and Keil observe that they come immediately after the murmuring over the spies’ report (Numbers 14:1), the oath that the rebels should fall in the wilderness, and the defeat at Hormah; in the very shadow of judgment, God gives laws for the worship of the land they had despaired of. They mark Henry’s observation that the law begins very encouragingly with “when ye come into the land,” a word full of grace; though the present generation should die in the wilderness, God assures their children that they would surely enter and worship there. They observe Calvin’s note on the character of the chapter: it partly repeats, and partly gathers into one place, precepts of sacrifice already given more fully or more scattered in Leviticus, ordering them afresh for the people. They note the comfort the witnesses find in the timing: that God did not cast off his people utterly for their rebellion, but renewed his covenant promise by these very laws, looking past the dying generation to the land he would still give. They observe that mercy rejoices over judgment, God speaking of grace where wrath might have been expected. They note that the offerings to come are tokens of reconciliation. The verse thus opens the gracious laws — addressed, in the next verse, to a people sure to enter the land.
2:
On Numbers 15:2 — “Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When ye be come into the land of your habitations, which I give unto you” — the witnesses mark the promise of the land renewed.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: some offerings were not meant for the wilderness but for the promised land, which afforded conveniences the desert did not. The Reformed Gill: spoken to the younger sort, those under twenty, who should enter, the older having been sworn to die in the wilderness. Wesley: “I give you” — will certainly give you, notwithstanding this great provocation.

The verse renews the promise. God speaks of the land he will surely give, when they come into it. The covenant gift is confirmed to the next generation.

So the witnesses see God renew, to the rising generation, the certain promise of the land their fathers had forfeited. The witnesses note the assurance in the words: “the land of your habitations, which I give unto you” — Wesley draws out that “I give” means “I will certainly give,” notwithstanding the great provocation of the spies’ rebellion; God repeats and amplifies the laws of sacrifice as a pledge that, through the appointed offerings, he would be reconciled to them and their children, and bring them in. They mark whom the word addresses, which Gill observes: chiefly the younger sort, those then under twenty, who alone of that host would live to enter; the older men, who had murmured, were sworn to die in the wilderness (Numbers 14:29), but their children were assured of the inheritance. They observe Clarke’s note that several of the offerings here prescribed were not meant to be kept in the wilderness, where the conveniences of settled agriculture (grain, wine, oil in plenty) were wanting, but in the promised land; the law looked forward to the time of their settled possession. They note the grace the witnesses find in this: that God, in the act of judging the rebels, comforted the faithful remnant with the renewed promise of Canaan; the gift was sure, the covenant unbroken. They observe that the laws of the land presuppose the giving of the land. They note that God’s faithfulness outlasts his people’s sin. The verse thus renews the promise: the land surely to be given — the offerings of the land specified in the next verses.
3:
On Numbers 15:3 — “And will make an offering by fire unto the LORD, a burnt offering, or a sacrifice in performing a vow… or in your solemn feasts” — the witnesses mark the occasions of the offerings.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke refers to the kinds of offerings in Leviticus 1:2. The Reformed Gill: the burnt offering wholly burnt, the others partly eaten; freewill, vow, or feast offerings. Wesley: “a sacrifice” here a peace offering.

The verse names the occasions. Offerings made by fire — in a vow, a freewill gift, or a feast. The kinds and occasions of sacrifice are set out.

So the witnesses see the various occasions named on which the appointed offerings, with their accompaniments, were to be brought. The witnesses note the kinds of sacrifice: “a burnt offering, or a sacrifice” — Gill distinguishes the burnt offering, wholly consumed upon the altar, from the other offerings (peace or thank offerings), of which part was eaten; Wesley takes “a sacrifice” here for the peace offering. They mark the occasions enumerated: “in performing a vow, or in a freewill offering, or in your solemn feasts” — the offerings might be brought in fulfilment of a vow, or as a free and voluntary gift of devotion, or at the appointed festivals of the year; the law covers the whole range of the people’s sacrifices, occasional and stated, vowed and free. They observe the end of all: “to make a sweet savour unto the LORD” — that the offering might be acceptable and well-pleasing to God, a fragrance of devotion rising to him. They note the witnesses’ reference back to Leviticus for the fuller laws of these sacrifices (Leviticus 1:2); the present chapter adds to them the rule of the accompanying meat and drink offerings. They observe that whatever the occasion, the sacrifice was to be offered in God’s appointed way, that it be accepted. They note the typology held throughout: the sweet savour points to Christ, whose offering was a sweet-smelling savour to God (Ephesians 5:2). The verse thus names the occasions of the offerings — the meat offering with a lamb following in the next verse.
4:
On Numbers 15:4 — “Then shall he that offereth his offering unto the LORD bring a meat offering of a tenth deal of flour mingled with the fourth part of an hin of oil” — the witnesses mark the meat offering with a lamb.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a meat offering of a tenth deal of flour with a fourth of an hin of oil, and a drink offering of wine. Charles Ellicott: the meal and drink offerings ordered with a lamb, in a vow, freewill offering, or feast. Wesley: a tenth deal is about five pints; an hin about five quarts.

The verse sets the smallest portion. With a lamb, a tenth of flour and a fourth-hin of oil. The meat offering accompanies the animal sacrifice.

So the witnesses see the rule begin with the meat offering appointed to accompany a lamb, the smallest of the sacrifices. The witnesses note the proportions: “a tenth deal of flour mingled with the fourth part of an hin of oil” — with the offering of a lamb, the meat offering was a tenth of an ephah of fine flour (about five pints, Wesley reckons) mixed with a quarter-hin of oil, and (the next verse adds) a like measure of wine for a drink offering. They mark the principle the witnesses draw out, which runs through the section: the animal sacrifice was not to be offered alone, but with its accompanying meat and drink offerings of flour, oil, and wine — the fruits of the field joined to the beast of the herd or flock, that the whole produce of the land be brought to God together. They observe Ellicott’s note that these accompaniments were ordered for the lamb offered in a vow, as a freewill offering, or at the solemn feasts. They note the meaning the witnesses find: the meat offering of flour and oil owned God as the giver of bread, and the drink offering of wine as the giver of gladness; the offerer brought the staples of life with his sacrifice, consecrating all to God. They observe the typology held throughout: the fine flour and oil and wine point to Christ, the bread and the true vine, and to the gladness of the Spirit. They note that the smallest sacrifice had its fixed accompaniment. The verse thus sets the meat offering with a lamb — the drink offering following in the next verse.
5:
On Numbers 15:5 — “And the fourth part of an hin of wine for a drink offering shalt thou prepare with the burnt offering or sacrifice, for one lamb” — the witnesses mark the drink offering with a lamb.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the quantity of meal and flour was augmented in proportion to the size of the sacrifice; with a lamb or kid, one tenth deal of flour and a fourth-hin of oil and wine. The Reformed Gill: wine of the grapes, as the Targum; no other sort might be used.

The verse adds the drink offering. A fourth-hin of wine is poured with the lamb. Wine is joined to flour and oil.

So the witnesses see the drink offering of wine appointed to accompany the lamb, completing its meat and drink offerings. The witnesses note the measure: “the fourth part of an hin of wine for a drink offering” — with a lamb, the wine poured out as a drink offering was a quarter-hin, the same measure as the oil of the meat offering. They mark Gill’s note that this was to be wine of the grapes, as the Targum specifies; no other sort or substitute might be used for the sacred libation, but the true fruit of the vine. They observe Clarke’s observation of the principle, here begun and carried through the section: the quantity of flour, oil, and wine was augmented in proportion to the size of the animal offered — least with a lamb, more with a ram, most with a bullock; the accessory offering grew with the principal sacrifice. They note the meaning the witnesses find in the drink offering: the wine poured out before the LORD owned him as the giver of the gladness of life, and (as the New Testament shows) figured the pouring out of the soul in devotion, and the blood of Christ poured out for many; Paul speaks of being himself poured out as a drink offering (Philippians 2:17). They observe that the lamb’s offering was now complete — flesh, flour, oil, and wine, the whole brought to God. They note that the smallest sacrifice wanted nothing of its due accompaniment. The verse thus adds the drink offering with a lamb — the larger measures for a ram following in the next verses.
6:
On Numbers 15:6 — “Or for a ram, thou shalt prepare for a meat offering two tenth deals of flour mingled with the third part of an hin of oil” — the witnesses mark the larger meat offering for a ram.

The Reformed Gill: for a ram, whether burnt or peace offering. Wesley: two tenth deals, because this belonged to a better sacrifice than the lamb; and in the next, for a bullock, three tenth deals — the accessory grows with the principal.

The verse raises the measure for a ram. Two tenths of flour and a third-hin of oil. The larger sacrifice gets the larger accompaniment.

So the witnesses see the meat offering increased for a ram, larger than that appointed for a lamb. The witnesses note the proportions: “two tenth deals of flour mingled with the third part of an hin of oil” — with a ram, the meat offering was doubled in flour (two tenths instead of one) and increased in oil (a third-hin instead of a fourth), and (the next verse adds) a like increase of wine. They mark the principle Wesley draws out plainly: the measure was greater because the ram was a better and larger sacrifice than the lamb; and so, for the still greater bullock, the measure would be greater yet (three tenth deals) — “the accessory sacrifice grows proportionably with the principal.” They observe the order and fitness the witnesses see in this: God appointed the accompanying offering to match the dignity and size of the victim, that the meat and drink offering should always be proportioned to the sacrifice it adorned; nothing was left to chance or whim, but all measured by rule. They note that the increase taught the offerer to honour the greater sacrifice with the greater gift of the field’s produce; the more was given to God, the more abundantly was the table of his altar furnished. They observe that the gradation — lamb, ram, bullock — ran in due proportion through flour, oil, and wine. They note that God’s worship was ordered with exactness and fitness. The verse thus raises the measure for a ram — the drink offering for it following in the next verse.
7:
On Numbers 15:7 — “And for a drink offering thou shalt offer the third part of an hin of wine, for a sweet savour unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the drink offering for a ram.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: a third part of an hin of wine for a drink offering, for a sweet savour. The Reformed Gill: the same quantity of wine as of oil in the meat offering.

The verse adds the drink offering for a ram. A third-hin of wine, for a sweet savour. The wine matches the increased oil.

So the witnesses see the drink offering for a ram set to match its larger meat offering. The witnesses note the measure: “the third part of an hin of wine” — Gill observes that the wine of the drink offering was the same quantity as the oil of the meat offering, here a third-hin for the ram, as it had been a fourth-hin for the lamb; the wine kept pace with the oil, and both with the size of the sacrifice. They mark the end declared: “for a sweet savour unto the LORD” — the whole offering, flesh and flour and oil and wine, was to ascend as a fragrance well-pleasing to God; the increase of the gift made not the acceptance, but the appointed order being kept, the offering was a sweet savour. They observe the consistency of the rule the witnesses have traced: the drink offering, like the meat offering, grew in proportion to the principal sacrifice, the ram’s exceeding the lamb’s and falling short of the bullock’s; God ordered the libation by the same fit gradation. They note the meaning of the poured-out wine, here repeated: it owned God as the giver of the gladness of the heart, and figured the devotion poured out before him, and the blood of Christ shed for the acceptance of his people. They observe that the ram’s offering was now complete in all its parts. They note that all was for the pleasing of God in his own appointed way. The verse thus adds the drink offering for a ram — the great measures for a bullock following in the next verses.
8:
On Numbers 15:8 — “And when thou preparest a bullock for a burnt offering, or for a sacrifice in performing a vow, or peace offerings unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the bullock and its occasions.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “in performing a vow” rather “in making a special vow” (Numbers 6:2). The Reformed Gill: a larger offering, requiring a larger meat and drink offering. Wesley: peace offerings, offered freely or by command, by way of gratitude to God.

The verse names the bullock. The greatest sacrifice, in a vow or peace offering, requires the greatest accompaniment. The largest victim is set forth.

So the witnesses see the rule come to the bullock, the largest of the sacrifices, with its occasions named. The witnesses note the victim and its uses: “a bullock for a burnt offering, or for a sacrifice in performing a vow, or peace offerings” — the bullock might be offered wholly as a burnt offering, or in fulfilment of a vow (Ellicott would render “a special vow,” as the Nazarite’s, Numbers 6:2), or as a peace offering. They mark Wesley’s note on the peace offerings: these were offered either freely or by command, and may be called peace or thank offerings by eminence, because brought purely by way of gratitude to God, with single respect to his goodness. They observe Gill’s point that the bullock, being the largest offering, required the largest meat and drink offering, as the next verses set out; the principle held that the accessory grew with the principal, and the greatest sacrifice had the fullest accompaniment. They note the fitness the witnesses see: that the costliest victim, the bullock of the herd, was honoured with the most abundant offering of flour, oil, and wine, the whole produce of the land brought in fullest measure to God. They observe the typology held throughout: the bullock wholly offered, like every victim, points to Christ, the great sacrifice, offered in fullest devotion. They note that the gradation of the offerings was now complete from lamb to bullock. The verse thus names the bullock and its occasions — its great meat offering following in the next verse.
9:
On Numbers 15:9 — “Then shall he bring with a bullock a meat offering of three tenth deals of flour mingled with half an hin of oil” — the witnesses mark the great meat offering for a bullock.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: with a bullock a meat offering of three tenth deals of flour with half an hin of oil, and half an hin of wine for a drink offering. The Reformed Gill: much larger than for a lamb or ram.

The verse sets the greatest measure. Three tenths of flour and half a hin of oil with a bullock. The largest accompaniment crowns the gradation.

So the witnesses see the meat offering reach its greatest measure with the bullock, completing the rising scale. The witnesses note the proportions: “three tenth deals of flour mingled with half an hin of oil” — with a bullock, the meat offering was three tenths of an ephah of flour and half a hin of oil, and (the next verse adds) half a hin of wine; the largest of the three measures, as the bullock was the largest of the three victims. They mark Gill’s observation that this was much larger than the offering appointed for a lamb (one tenth) or a ram (two tenths); the scale rose by even steps — one, two, three tenths of flour; a fourth, a third, a half of a hin of oil and wine — in fit proportion to the sacrifice. They observe the order and fitness the witnesses have traced through the whole section: God ordained the accompanying offering to grow with the principal, so that the greatest victim had the fullest gift of the field’s produce; the worship of God was framed with exact and beautiful proportion. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that our gifts to God should answer to the greatness of his mercies and of our offerings; the more we bring, the more abundantly should our devotion overflow. They observe the typology held throughout: the abundant flour, oil, and wine with the bullock point to the fulness of grace in Christ, the great sacrifice. They note that the gradation was now complete and crowned. The verse thus sets the great meat offering for a bullock — its drink offering following in the next verse.
10:
On Numbers 15:10 — “And thou shalt bring for a drink offering half an hin of wine, for an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the great drink offering for a bullock.

The Reformed Gill: the same quantity of wine as of oil — half an hin — for the drink offering with a bullock.

The verse adds the great drink offering. Half a hin of wine with the bullock, for a sweet savour. The wine completes the fullest offering.

So the witnesses see the drink offering reach its greatest measure with the bullock, matching its great meat offering. The witnesses note the measure: “half an hin of wine” — Gill observes that the wine of the drink offering was the same quantity as the oil of the meat offering, here half a hin for the bullock, the largest of the three measures; the wine kept pace with the oil and both with the sacrifice. They mark the end declared: “for an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD” — the whole, flesh and flour and oil and wine, ascended as a fragrance well-pleasing to God; the fullest offering, brought in God’s appointed order, was a sweet savour. They observe that with this the gradation of the offerings was complete: lamb, ram, and bullock, each with its proportioned meat and drink offering, the wine rising from a fourth to a third to a half of a hin; the worship of God ordered throughout by fit and even measure. They note the meaning of the poured-out wine, here at its fullest: it owned God as the giver of the gladness of life, and figured the abundant devotion poured out before him, and the blood of Christ shed in fullest measure for his people. They observe that nothing of the bullock’s offering was wanting, the whole produce of the land brought with the costliest victim. They note that the greatest sacrifice closed in the sweetest savour. The verse thus adds the great drink offering for a bullock — the rule summed in the next verses.
11:
On Numbers 15:11 — “Thus shall it be done for one bullock, or for one ram, or for a lamb, or a kid” — the witnesses mark the rule summed for every victim.

The Reformed Gill: so much flour, oil, and wine for each, making no difference whether the beast were young or old.

The verse sums the rule. The appointed measures hold for bullock, ram, lamb, or kid. Every sacrifice has its set accompaniment.

So the witnesses see the rule of the meat and drink offerings gathered into a summary covering every kind of victim. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: “thus shall it be done for one bullock, or for one ram, or for a lamb, or a kid” — the measures already given were to be observed for each sort of animal sacrifice, the bullock with its three tenths, the ram with two, the lamb or kid with one, each with its proportioned oil and wine. They mark Gill’s observation that no difference was made on account of the age of the beast, whether young or old; the rule went by the kind of victim, not its years, the same accompaniment serving for each of its sort. They observe the order and certainty the witnesses see in this: God left nothing to the offerer’s guess, but fixed for every sacrifice its exact meat and drink offering, that all might be done decently and in order; the worshipper knew always what to bring. They note that the kid is here named with the lamb, the lesser cattle of the flock sharing the smallest measure; every clean beast offered had its appointed gift of the field joined to it. They observe that the rule, thus summed, was plain and complete, covering all the sacrifices of the people. They note that God’s worship was furnished with exactness, that none might offer too little or too much, but each according to the rule. They observe that the law provided for every case. The verse thus sums the rule for every victim — its application by number stated in the next verse.

So the witnesses see, in this summary, the order and care with which God framed even the lesser details of his worship. They observe that no sacrifice, of whatever kind or size, was to be offered bare, but each was to be attended by its appointed meat and drink offering, the fruits of the field joined to the beast of the herd or flock; the whole produce of the land was thus brought to God together. They note the equity of the rule that went by the kind of victim and not its age, so that a young beast and an old of the same sort had the same accompaniment, and none need be in doubt what to bring. They mark that God left nothing to the offerer’s guess or fancy, but fixed all by certain measure, that his service might be done decently and in order, and that none might offer too little in niggardliness or too much in self-will. They observe that the law, thus plainly summed, provided for every case, the lamb and the kid no less than the bullock, that the worship of God might want nothing of its due.
12:
On Numbers 15:12 — “According to the number that ye shall prepare, so shall ye do to every one according to their number” — the witnesses mark the offerings reckoned by the number of victims.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: according to the number prepared, so to every one. The Reformed Gill: in proportion to the number of the cattle, so the quantity of the meat and drink offerings. Wesley: as many cattle as ye sacrifice, so many meal and drink offerings.

The verse multiplies by number. Each victim has its own meat and drink offering, however many. The rule scales to the number offered.

So the witnesses see the rule extended to cover any number of victims, each with its own accompaniment. The witnesses note the principle: “according to the number that ye shall prepare, so shall ye do to every one” — Gill and Wesley draw out that the meat and drink offerings were to be multiplied according to the number of the animals sacrificed: as many beasts as were offered, so many meal and drink offerings were to be brought, each victim having its own full accompaniment of flour, oil, and wine. They mark that the rule was not only for a single sacrifice but for many: at the great feasts, when many bullocks, rams, and lambs were offered together, each was to be attended by its proper meat and drink offering, reckoned by its kind and counted by the number. They observe the order and exactness the witnesses see: God would have his worship furnished in full, no victim offered without its appointed gift of the field, however great the multitude of sacrifices; the rule scaled to the occasion without confusion. They note the lesson the witnesses draw: that the abundance of God’s mercies calls for abundance in our devotion; as the sacrifices multiplied, so did the offerings of thanksgiving. They observe that nothing was left undetermined, the law providing for one victim or many alike. They note that God’s service was ordered to the last particular. The verse thus reckons the offerings by number — the same rule for native and stranger following in the next verses.
13:
On Numbers 15:13 — “All that are born of the country shall do these things after this manner, in offering an offering made by fire, of a sweet savour unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the rule binding on the native Israelite.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: the reference is to the indigenous Israelites, the home-born, as distinguished from the stranger of Numbers 15:14. The Reformed Gill: all Israelites should with their sacrifices bring their meat and drink offerings of the quantity directed.

The verse binds the native. Every home-born Israelite is to offer in this manner. The rule is the standard for all the people.

So the witnesses see the rule of the meat and drink offerings made binding upon every native-born Israelite. The witnesses note the obligation: “all that are born of the country shall do these things after this manner” — Ellicott identifies these as the indigenous Israelites, the home-born of the nation, as distinguished from the stranger named in the next verse; Gill notes that all Israelites, in offering their sacrifices, were to bring with them the meat and drink offerings in the quantity directed. They mark that the rule was no mere counsel but a binding statute for the whole people: none of the native-born was exempt; whoever offered a sacrifice must offer it in this appointed manner, with its proper accompaniment of flour, oil, and wine. They observe the end repeated: “of a sweet savour unto the LORD” — the offering, so brought, was acceptable and well-pleasing to God; the appointed order made it a fragrance to him. They note that the law thus established a uniform manner of worship for all Israel, that the service of God might be one and the same throughout the nation, none offering after his own fashion but all after God’s. They observe the fitness of this uniformity: the one God was to be worshipped in one appointed way by all his people. They note that the standard set for the native would, in the next verse, be extended to the stranger also. They observe that God’s worship admits no private innovation. The verse thus binds the native to the rule — the same rule given to the stranger in the next verses.
14:
On Numbers 15:14 — “And if a stranger sojourn with you… and will offer an offering made by fire… as ye do, so he shall do” — the witnesses mark the stranger’s offering like the native’s.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the stranger’s offering, as a sweet savour, to be done as the native does. Adam Clarke: a stranger sojourning (Leviticus 19:33). The Reformed Gill and Calvin: not every stranger, but the proselyte of righteousness, descended from heathen nations yet turned to God and received into the Church; the uncircumcised excluded.

The verse admits the stranger. The proselyte who offers is to do as the native does. The convert worships on the same terms.

So the witnesses see the law open the same worship to the sojourning stranger who had joined himself to the LORD. The witnesses note the provision: “if a stranger sojourn with you… as ye do, so he shall do” — the foreigner dwelling among Israel who would offer a sacrifice was to bring it in the very same manner, with the same meat and drink offerings, as the native-born; there was no lesser rule for him. They mark the careful definition the witnesses give of this “stranger”: not, as Gill and Calvin insist, every foreigner whatever, but the proselyte of righteousness — one who, sprung from the heathen nations, had professedly turned from idols to the true God and been received, by circumcision, into the body of the covenant people; the uncircumcised stranger, remaining in his heathenism, was excluded from offering. They observe Clarke’s note pointing to the kindred laws of the stranger’s welcome (Leviticus 19:33). They note the great truth the witnesses see foreshadowed here: that the convert from the nations was admitted to the same worship and the same acceptance as the native Israelite, a presage (as Wesley will say at v. 15) of the future calling of the Gentiles into the one Church of God (Ephesians 2:13). They observe that God’s house was open to all who came to him in his appointed way, of whatever nation. They note that grace knew no national bound. The verse thus admits the stranger to the same offering — the oneness of the ordinance pressed in the next verses.
15:
On Numbers 15:15 — “One ordinance shall be both for you of the congregation, and also for the stranger that sojourneth with you… as ye are, so shall the stranger be before the LORD” — the witnesses mark one ordinance for native and stranger.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: literally, as for the congregation, there shall be one ordinance for you and for the stranger. The Reformed Gill: one and the same precept. Wesley: “before the LORD” — his sacrifices offered in the same manner and accepted on the same terms; a presage of the future calling of the Gentiles.

The verse makes one law for both. The same ordinance binds native and stranger before the LORD. Native and convert stand alike in worship.

So the witnesses see the worship of God made one and the same for the native Israelite and the proselyte stranger. The witnesses note the equality: “one ordinance shall be both for you of the congregation, and also for the stranger… as ye are, so shall the stranger be before the LORD” — there was not one rule for the home-born and another for the convert, but one and the same ordinance for both; the stranger who had joined the covenant people worshipped on the same terms and was accepted on the same footing. They mark Wesley’s drawing out of the words “before the LORD”: as to the worship of God, the proselyte’s sacrifices were to be offered in the same manner and accepted by God on the same terms as the native’s; and this, he says, was a presage of the future calling of the Gentiles, when the partition wall should be broken down and Jew and Gentile made one in Christ (Galatians 3:28). They observe the breadth of grace the witnesses see here: that even under the law, which so marked off Israel from the nations, the door stood open to the stranger who turned to God, and he was made equal in worship to the native. They note the foreshadowing of the gospel, in which there is neither Jew nor Greek, but one body and one Spirit, all accepted alike in the Beloved. They observe that God is no respecter of persons or of nations, but receives all who come to him aright. They note that the one ordinance bound a people of many origins into one worship. The verse thus makes one law for both — the oneness restated in the next verse.
16:
On Numbers 15:16 — “One law and one manner shall be for you, and for the stranger that sojourneth with you” — the witnesses mark one law and one manner for all.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: one law and one manner for you and the stranger. The Reformed Gill: one law respecting these sacrifices, and one manner of offering them; one and the same precept and one and the same punishment for non-observance.

The verse repeats the oneness. One law and one manner bind native and stranger alike. The equality is sealed by repetition.

So the witnesses see the oneness of the ordinance pressed again, that native and stranger stood under one law and one manner of worship. The witnesses note the double term: “one law and one manner” — Gill distinguishes them as one and the same precept respecting these sacrifices, and one and the same way of offering them; both the rule and the rite were identical for the home-born and the convert, and (he adds) one and the same punishment awaited the breach of it in either. They mark the emphasis of the repetition: the law has now said three times over (vv. 14–16) that the stranger who joined himself to Israel should worship exactly as the native; the matter is driven home, that none might think the convert a worshipper of lesser standing or bound by a lighter rule. They observe the truth the witnesses see sealed by this: that in the worship and acceptance of God there was perfect equality between the Israelite and the proselyte; the same law bound them, the same manner united them, the same God received them. They note the gospel foreshadowed once more: one law and one manner for Jew and Gentile prefigures the one body, one faith, one baptism of the church (Ephesians 4:5), in which all the redeemed, of every nation, are made one. They observe that God’s house is governed by one rule for all who enter it. They note that the unity of worship answered to the unity of God. The verse thus seals one law and one manner for all — the law then turning, in the next verses, to the cake of the first dough.
17:
On Numbers 15:17 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the new word introducing the cake of the first dough.

The Reformed Gill: the LORD continued to speak to Moses, for the following law was given at the same time as those before.

The verse opens a new precept. God speaks again to give the law of the first dough. A fresh ordinance begins.

So the witnesses see a fresh divine word open the law of the heave offering of the first dough. The witnesses note the formula, brief as it is: “the LORD spake unto Moses” — Gill observes that God continued to speak, the following law being given at the same time as the laws of the meat and drink offerings just delivered; the new preface marks a distinct precept, yet of the same occasion and the same gracious purpose. They mark that this law, like the foregoing, looked forward to the land: it concerned the bread made from the corn of Canaan, and so belonged to the time of their settled possession, not the wilderness. They observe the fitness of its placing: having ordained the offerings of flour, oil, and wine to accompany the sacrifices, God now adds the offering of the first of the dough, that the very bread of the people’s table should also own him as its giver. They note the manner of God’s legislation the witnesses observe: he gives his statutes one by one, each with its own word, that their distinctness and authority be felt; the people received not a confused mass but an ordered series of commands. They observe that the new word prepares for a law of firstfruits in another form — the cake of the first dough. They note that God claims the first of all his gifts. The verse thus opens the new precept — the offering of the first dough commanded in the next verses.

So the witnesses see, in this brief preface, the steady method of God’s legislation: that he gives his statutes one by one, each with its own word, that their distinctness and authority be felt. They observe that the law of the first dough, like the offerings before it, looked forward to the land, concerning the bread that should be made from the corn of Canaan, and so belonged to the time of settled possession rather than the wilderness, where they ate the manna and had no harvest of their own. They note the fitness of its placing: having ordained the offerings of flour, oil, and wine to accompany the sacrifices, God now adds the offering of the first of the dough, that the very bread of the people’s daily table should also own him as its giver. They mark that God claims the first of all his gifts, the firstfruits of the field and the first of the kneaded loaf alike, that his people might be kept ever mindful that all they had came from his hand. They observe that the new word, brief as it is, opens a fresh token of dependence and thankfulness woven into the household’s daily bread.
18:
On Numbers 15:18 — “Speak unto the children of Israel, and say unto them, When ye come into the land whither I bring you” — the witnesses mark the law bound to the land.

The Reformed Gill: spoken to the children of Israel only, who alone were bound to the law of the cake of the first dough, and not the Gentiles; the dough of a Gentile was free from the cake.

The verse ties the law to the land. The offering of the first dough is for when they come into Canaan. The law awaits the settled possession.

So the witnesses see the law of the first dough, like the offerings before it, bound to the time of Israel’s coming into the land. The witnesses note the forward look: “when ye come into the land whither I bring you” — the offering of the cake belonged to the settled life of Canaan, when Israel should sow and reap and grind and bake the corn of their own fields; it could not be kept in the wilderness, where they ate the manna and had no harvest of their own. They mark the renewed assurance in the words “whither I bring you”: as in the opening of the chapter, God speaks of the land as certainly to be possessed, the promise standing firm to the rising generation despite the rebellion of their fathers. They observe Gill’s note on whom the law bound: the children of Israel only, the covenant people, were obliged to offer the cake of the first dough; the dough of a Gentile was free from it, the law of firstfruits belonging to those who held the land as God’s gift. They note the fitness the witnesses see: that the people who would eat the bread of Canaan, given them by God, should first own him as the giver by offering the first of the dough; the gift of the land carried the obligation of its firstfruits. They observe that the law looked beyond the present judgment to the people’s settled and fruitful possession. They note that God’s claim followed his gift. The verse thus ties the law to the land — the offering itself commanded in the next verse.
19:
On Numbers 15:19 — “Then it shall be, that when ye eat of the bread of the land, ye shall offer up an heave offering unto the LORD” — the witnesses mark the offering before eating the bread of the land.

The Reformed Gill: when they ate of the bread of Canaan, before they actually did, when they had ground the corn and mixed the dough. Wesley: “when ye eat” — when you are about to eat it, for before they ate they were to offer this; “the bread” is the bread-corn.

The verse sets the time. Before eating the bread of the land, they offer a heave offering. God’s portion comes first.

So the witnesses see the offering of the first dough required before the people ate of the bread of the land. The witnesses note the timing: “when ye eat of the bread of the land, ye shall offer up an heave offering” — Gill and Wesley draw out that “when ye eat” means “when ye are about to eat,” before they actually partook; when the corn of Canaan had been ground into flour and mixed into dough, before any of it was eaten, the first of it was to be offered to the LORD. They mark the kind of offering: a “heave offering,” lifted up and set apart for God and his priests, as a token that the whole was his and from him. They observe the principle the witnesses see, the same that governed all the firstfruits: God’s portion came first, before the people took their own use of the bread; to eat first and offer after would be to forget the giver in the gift. They note Wesley’s clarification that “the bread” here means the bread-corn, the produce of the harvest from which the dough was made; the offering was of the first of the new crop, made into a cake. They observe the lesson the witnesses draw: that all our enjoyment of God’s gifts should begin with the acknowledgment of him, the firstfruits hallowing the whole; the believer eats his bread with thanksgiving, owning God first. They note that the bread of the land was to be sanctified by the offering of its first. The verse thus sets the time before eating — the cake itself named in the next verse.
20:
On Numbers 15:20 — “Ye shall offer up a cake of the first of your dough for an heave offering… so shall ye heave it” — the witnesses mark the cake of the first dough.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “the first of your dough,” or mixed meal (arisoth, used only in the plural, found also in Nehemiah 10:37). The Reformed Gill: a cake of the first dough, of the first corn threshed and ground. Calvin: another kind of firstfruits, more lively in the bread itself. Adam Clarke refers to the firstfruits of Exodus 22:29.

The verse names the cake. A cake of the first dough is heaved to the LORD. The first of the bread is given to God.

So the witnesses see the offering of the first dough made in the form of a cake, lifted up to the LORD. The witnesses note the gift: “a cake of the first of your dough for an heave offering” — Gill explains that of the first dough, made from the first corn threshed, winnowed, and ground, they were to make a cake and offer it as a heave offering to the LORD; the offering was of the bread itself, not merely the grain. They mark the word Ellicott notes, arisoth, “dough” or “mixed meal,” a rare term found elsewhere only in Nehemiah and Ezekiel of this same offering. They observe Calvin’s reflection on why the offering was of the dough and not only of the ears: there was here another kind of firstfruits, and the acknowledgment of God was the more lively and homely in the bread itself, the very food of daily life, than in the raw grain; the people owned God as the giver of their bread. They note the meaning the witnesses see, which the New Testament draws out: “if the firstfruit be holy, the lump is also holy” (Romans 11:16); the offering of the first cake sanctified the whole baking and, in the figure, the whole people. They observe the typology: the firstfruits point to Christ, the firstfruits of his people, and to the consecration of all our substance to God. They note that the first and best of the daily bread belonged to the LORD. The verse thus names the cake of the first dough — its perpetual obligation stated in the next verse.
21:
On Numbers 15:21 — “Of the first of your dough ye shall give unto the LORD an heave offering in your generations” — the witnesses mark the perpetual offering of the first dough.

The Reformed Gill: of the first dough they were to give to the LORD, as an acknowledgment of his being the sovereign Lord and owner of the land of Canaan, and by way of thankfulness.

The verse makes the offering perpetual. The first of the dough is to be given throughout their generations. The acknowledgment of God endures.

So the witnesses see the offering of the first dough made a perpetual ordinance for all generations of Israel. The witnesses note the lasting obligation: “ye shall give unto the LORD an heave offering in your generations” — the offering of the first cake was not for one time only but to be rendered throughout their generations, as often as they baked the bread of the land. They mark the reasons Gill draws out for the offering: it was an acknowledgment that the LORD was the sovereign Lord and possessor of heaven and earth, and the true owner and proprietor of the land of Canaan, who had given it to Israel; and it was rendered by way of thankfulness for his bounty, the people owning that their daily bread came from his hand. They observe the fitness of so homely and constant an offering: as bread was the staple of daily life, baked again and again, so the offering of its first was a continual, repeated confession of dependence on God, woven into the ordinary round of the household. They note that the gift went, as a heave offering, to the priests, that those who served the altar might live of the firstfruits of the people (Nehemiah 10:37). They observe the lesson the witnesses see: that God is to be owned and thanked not only at the great feasts but in the daily bread, and that the first and best of all we have is his. They note that the perpetual statute kept the people ever mindful of the giver. The verse thus makes the offering perpetual — the law turning, in the next verses, to sins of ignorance.
22:
On Numbers 15:22 — “And if ye have erred, and not observed all these commandments, which the LORD hath spoken unto Moses” — the witnesses mark the case of sins of ignorance.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: rather, “if ye shall err and not observe.” The Puritan Henry: the laws of sacrifice for sins of ignorance, which the Jews understand of false worship through the error of their teachers. The Reformed Gill: every sin is an error, a missing of the mark; Jarchi and the Jews interpret of idolatry. Calvin: he enumerates two of the four classes of Leviticus. Wesley: these commandments, concerning the outward service of God.

The verse opens the case of error. If they err and fail to keep the commandments, provision is made. Sins of ignorance are met with atonement.

So the witnesses see the law turn to the case of sins committed in ignorance, and the atonement provided for them. The witnesses note the supposition: “if ye have erred, and not observed all these commandments” — Ellicott would render it “if ye shall err and not observe”; Gill marks that every sin is in truth an error, a missing of the mark, a wandering from the way of God’s commandments. They mark the interpretation the Jewish writers and Henry give: that this passage chiefly concerns false worship or idolatry fallen into through the error of their teachers, a national failing in the service of God; Wesley too takes the commandments here as those concerning the outward service and ceremonies of God, wherein this law differs from the like provision of Leviticus 4:13. They observe Calvin’s note that Moses here enumerates only two of the four classes of sin offering treated in Leviticus — that of the whole congregation and that of the individual — gathering the law afresh. They note the gracious assumption of the passage: that God’s people will sometimes err, not in defiant rebellion, but in ignorance and inadvertence, failing to keep all his commandments; and that he has made provision of atonement even for such. They observe the comfort the witnesses find: that sins of weakness and ignorance, which the holiest are not free from, do not cut off from God, but are met by the appointed sacrifice. They note the contrast soon to come with presumptuous sin. The verse thus opens the case of error — the atonement for the congregation following in the next verses.
23:
On Numbers 15:23 — “Even all that the LORD hath commanded you by the hand of Moses… and henceforward among your generations” — the witnesses mark the whole body of the law in view.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “henceforward” rather “and onward,” or thenceforward; nothing in the word marks whether later legislation is meant. The Reformed Gill: all the laws given to Israel by Moses, moral, ceremonial, or judicial.

The verse names the whole law. All that God commanded by Moses, throughout their generations, is in view. The provision covers the entire law.

So the witnesses see the atonement for ignorance extended to cover any breach of the whole body of the law God had given by Moses. The witnesses note the comprehensiveness: “all that the LORD hath commanded you by the hand of Moses” — Gill takes this to embrace the whole compass of the laws given to Israel through Moses, whether moral, ceremonial, or judicial, recorded in this book and the books before; the provision for sins of ignorance reached to error in any of them. They mark the temporal phrase: “from the day that the LORD commanded… and henceforward among your generations” — Ellicott would render “and onward,” or thenceforward, noting that nothing in the word itself determines whether later legislation is included; the law looked from the day of its giving forward through all the generations of Israel. They observe the breadth of grace the witnesses see: that God’s mercy in providing atonement for ignorance was not limited to a few commandments but covered the whole law, so that whatever the people transgressed unwittingly, there was a sacrifice to cleanse it. They note that the law itself acknowledged the people’s frailty and made constant provision for it; the same Moses who delivered the commands delivered also the remedy for their unwitting breach. They observe that the whole law, in all its parts, stood under this mercy. They note that no sin of ignorance was left without its appointed atonement. The verse thus names the whole law in view — the offering for the congregation’s error following in the next verse.
24:
On Numbers 15:24 — “Then it shall be, if ought be committed by ignorance without the knowledge of the congregation… a young bullock for a burnt offering… and one kid of the goats for a sin offering” — the witnesses mark the offering for the congregation’s error.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: the case here refers to the whole congregation, the cases above to the individual (Leviticus 4:2). Charles Ellicott: shegagah, transgressions committed unwittingly, in contrast to sins committed presumptuously (Numbers 15:30). The Reformed Gill: ignorance of the law, not understanding it or its precepts.

The verse appoints the offering. For the congregation’s unwitting sin, a bullock and a kid. The whole people’s error is atoned.

So the witnesses see the sacrifice appointed for a sin of ignorance committed by the whole congregation. The witnesses note the case: “if ought be committed by ignorance without the knowledge of the congregation” — a transgression of the law fallen into by the people as a body, unwittingly, without their knowing it to be sin; Gill explains it as ignorance of the law, not understanding its precepts or the manner of obedience. They mark the contrast Ellicott draws out from the Hebrew shegagah: a transgression committed unwittingly, by error and inadvertence, as opposed to the presumptuous, high-handed sin of verse 30; the whole passage turns on this distinction between the sin of weakness and the sin of defiance. They observe the offering commanded: “a young bullock for a burnt offering… and one kid of the goats for a sin offering” — for the congregation’s error, a bullock was offered whole to God in consecration, and a kid as a sin offering for atonement, with their meat and drink offerings. They note Clarke’s observation that this case concerns the whole congregation, where the earlier laws of Leviticus had dealt also with the individual (Leviticus 4:2); the public sin of the nation needed a public atonement. They observe the typology the witnesses see: the sin offering for the people points to Christ, the propitiation for the sins of the whole body of his people. They note that even a whole nation’s unwitting sin found mercy. The verse thus appoints the offering for the congregation’s error — the atonement and forgiveness following in the next verse.
25:
On Numbers 15:25 — “And the priest shall make an atonement for all the congregation… and it shall be forgiven them; for it is ignorance” — the witnesses mark the atonement and forgiveness of the congregation.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: even sins committed through ignorance required an atonement, and God in mercy provided one. Charles Ellicott: rather, “for it is a sin of ignorance, or an error.” The Reformed Gill: a sin offering, a type of Christ, the propitiation not only for the Jews but throughout the world.

The verse grants forgiveness. The priest atones, and the congregation is forgiven, for it was ignorance. Mercy meets the unwitting sin.

So the witnesses see the congregation atoned for and forgiven, the sin of ignorance covered by the appointed sacrifice. The witnesses note the priestly act: “the priest shall make an atonement for all the congregation” — by offering the sin offering, the priest made atonement on behalf of the whole people; Gill marks this sin offering as a type of Christ, the propitiation not only for the sins of Israel but for those of his people throughout the world (1 John 2:2). They mark the grant of pardon: “and it shall be forgiven them” — upon the atonement made, the people were forgiven, the guilt of their unwitting sin remitted; the sacrifice availed, and God put away the offence. They observe the ground given: “for it is ignorance” — Ellicott would render it “for it is a sin of ignorance, or an error”; because the sin was not of defiance but of inadvertence, it was capable of this atonement and forgiveness. They note the great truth Clarke draws out: that even sins committed through ignorance required an atonement — ignorance did not make them no sins — yet God in his mercy provided the atonement they required; the unwitting sinner was neither excused without sacrifice nor left without remedy. They observe the typology the witnesses press: the priest’s atonement and the people’s forgiveness point to Christ, through whose sacrifice the sins of his people, known and unknown, are pardoned. They note that mercy met the sin of weakness. The verse thus grants forgiveness to the congregation — its certainty repeated in the next verse.
26:
On Numbers 15:26 — “And it shall be forgiven all the congregation… and the stranger that sojourneth among them; seeing all the people were in ignorance” — the witnesses mark the forgiveness extended to the stranger.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: rather, “for in regard to all the people, it was done in ignorance, or unwittingly.” The Reformed Gill: repeated for the certainty of it, and for the sake of what follows.

The verse repeats the pardon. The forgiveness reaches the congregation and the stranger, since all sinned in ignorance. Mercy covers all the people.

So the witnesses see the forgiveness of the congregation repeated and extended to the sojourning stranger among them. The witnesses note the repetition: “it shall be forgiven all the congregation” — Gill observes that the grant of pardon is said again for the certainty of it, that the people might be assured the atonement availed and the sin was put away; God doubles his word of mercy that faith may rest on it. They mark the inclusion of the stranger: “and the stranger that sojourneth among them” — the proselyte who dwelt with Israel shared in the atonement and the forgiveness, as he shared in the worship and the law; the mercy, like the ordinance, was one for native and convert alike. They observe the ground repeated: “seeing all the people were in ignorance” — Ellicott would render it “for in regard to all the people, it was done unwittingly”; because the whole body had sinned not in defiance but in ignorance, the atonement reached them all, the home-born and the stranger together. They note the breadth of grace the witnesses see: that the same forgiveness which covered the native Israelite covered also the stranger who had joined himself to the people of God; none who belonged to the congregation was shut out of the mercy. They observe the foreshadowing of the gospel, in which the one atonement of Christ avails for all his people of every nation. They note that the certainty of pardon was made sure by the repeated word. The verse thus extends the forgiveness to the stranger — the like atonement for the individual following in the next verses.
27:
On Numbers 15:27 — “And if any soul sin through ignorance, then he shall bring a she goat of the first year for a sin offering” — the witnesses mark the offering for the individual’s error.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: one law for native and stranger in sins of omission; a she goat for a sin offering. Charles Ellicott: no restriction here, as in Leviticus 4:27, to sins of commission. The Reformed Gill: any private person breaking a command, not rightly understanding it.

The verse turns to the individual. For one soul’s unwitting sin, a she-goat for a sin offering. The single person’s error too is atoned.

So the witnesses see the law provide, as for the congregation, so for the single soul that sins in ignorance. The witnesses note the case: “if any soul sin through ignorance” — Gill explains it of any private or particular person who breaks a command, not rightly understanding it, or not adverting to the circumstances required in obeying it; the individual’s unwitting transgression. They mark Ellicott’s observation that here, unlike the parallel in Leviticus 4:27, there is no restriction to sins of commission; the provision covers sins of omission as well, the failure to do what was commanded as much as the doing of what was forbidden, when fallen into through ignorance. They observe the offering appointed: “a she goat of the first year for a sin offering” — a smaller and humbler victim than the bullock and kid of the congregation, fitting the single offerer; yet a true sin offering, availing for atonement. They note the equity the witnesses see, drawn out in the next verses: the same law held for the native and the stranger, the individual Israelite and the sojourning convert, in the matter of these sins of ignorance. They observe the comfort the witnesses find: that God made provision not only for the great public sins of the nation but for the private, unwitting failings of the single soul; none was left without a remedy. They note the typology: the sin offering points to Christ, who atones for the secret faults of each of his people. The verse thus appoints the offering for the individual’s error — the atonement and forgiveness following in the next verse.
28:
On Numbers 15:28 — “And the priest shall make an atonement for the soul that sinneth ignorantly… and it shall be forgiven him” — the witnesses mark the atonement and forgiveness of the individual.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: the priest shall make atonement for the soul that sinneth by ignorance before the LORD, and it shall be forgiven him. The Reformed Gill: by offering his sin offering for him.

The verse grants the individual pardon. The priest atones for the soul that sinned in ignorance, and he is forgiven. Mercy reaches the single sinner.

So the witnesses see the single soul that sinned in ignorance atoned for and forgiven, as the congregation had been. The witnesses note the priestly act: “the priest shall make an atonement for the soul that sinneth ignorantly” — Gill observes that the priest made atonement by offering the appointed sin offering for him; the same priestly mediation that availed for the whole people availed also for the individual. They mark the grant of pardon: “and it shall be forgiven him” — upon the atonement made, the unwitting sinner was forgiven, his guilt remitted; the sacrifice availed for the single soul as truly as for the nation. They observe the phrase Keil and Delitzsch note, “when he sinneth by ignorance before the LORD” — the sin, though hidden from the man himself in his ignorance, was before the LORD, who saw it; yet, being of ignorance and not defiance, it was covered by the atonement. They note the tender equity the witnesses see: that God dealt with the single soul as carefully as with the congregation, providing for each the same atonement and the same forgiveness; the humblest individual’s unwitting sin was met by the same mercy as the nation’s. They observe the typology the witnesses press: the priest’s atonement points to Christ, the great High Priest, who makes atonement for the secret and unwitting sins of each believer, so that they are forgiven. They note that mercy reached every soul that sinned in weakness. The verse thus grants the individual pardon — the one law for native and stranger restated in the next verse.
29:
On Numbers 15:29 — “Ye shall have one law for him that sinneth through ignorance, both for him that is born among the children of Israel, and for the stranger that sojourneth among them” — the witnesses mark one law of atonement for native and stranger.

The Reformed Gill: one law, enjoining a she goat for a sin offering, for both the native and the proselyte who sinned ignorantly.

The verse makes one law of pardon. The same atonement for native and stranger who sin in ignorance. Mercy, like the ordinance, is one for all.

So the witnesses see the atonement for sins of ignorance, like the worship before it, made one and the same for the native Israelite and the sojourning stranger. The witnesses note the equality: “ye shall have one law for him that sinneth through ignorance, both for him that is born among the children of Israel, and for the stranger” — Gill observes that one and the same law, enjoining the she-goat for a sin offering, served for both the home-born and the proselyte who had sinned unwittingly; there was no different rule of atonement for the convert. They mark how the chapter has held this equality throughout: as there was one ordinance for native and stranger in the offerings (vv. 15–16), so now there is one law of forgiveness for native and stranger in their sins of ignorance; the convert was equal not only in worship but in mercy. They observe the breadth of grace the witnesses see: that the stranger who had joined himself to the LORD was met, when he sinned in weakness, by the same atonement and the same pardon as the native; God’s mercy knew no national bound within his people. They note the foreshadowing of the gospel, in which the one sacrifice of Christ avails alike for Jew and Gentile, and there is one way of forgiveness for all who believe (Romans 3:30). They observe that the unity of the law of pardon answered to the unity of the people of God. They note that mercy was as impartial as the ordinance. The verse thus makes one law of pardon for all — before the law turns, in sharp contrast, to presumptuous sin.
30:
On Numbers 15:30 — “But the soul that doeth ought presumptuously… the same reproacheth the LORD; and that soul shall be cut off from among his people” — the witnesses mark the presumptuous sin and its doom.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: literally, “with a high hand”; “reproacheth” rather “blasphemeth” (2 Kings 19:22). The Puritan Henry: presumptuous sinners avowedly confront God’s authority. Adam Clarke: bold daring transgression against the fullest evidence admitted no atonement. The Reformed Gill and Calvin: “with a high hand,” through pride, in a stubborn, daring way. Wesley: he sets God at defiance.

The verse condemns the high-handed sinner. He that sins presumptuously reproaches the LORD and is cut off. Defiant sin finds no atonement.

So the witnesses see, set in sharp contrast to the sins of ignorance, the presumptuous sin that has no sacrifice. The witnesses note the phrase: “doeth ought presumptuously” — literally, as Ellicott and Gill render it, “with a high hand,” through pride; the Targum says “with an uncovered head,” openly and insolently; Henry describes such as avowedly confront God’s authority and set up their own will against his, sinning not in weakness or ignorance but in deliberate, daring defiance. They mark the heinousness declared: “the same reproacheth the LORD” — Ellicott would render it “blasphemeth”; Wesley draws out that such a one sets God at defiance and exposes him to contempt, as if he were unable to punish transgressors; the high-handed sin is an affront and a reproach to God himself. They observe the dreadful sentence: “that soul shall be cut off from among his people” — for such sin, Clarke notes, there was no atonement appointed; bold transgression against the fullest light and in despite of the divine authority admitted of no sacrifice, but the sinner was to be cut off, excluded from God’s people and presence. They note the great distinction the witnesses press throughout the passage: between the sin of ignorance, which the appointed sacrifice could cleanse, and the sin of presumption, which it could not; the difference lay not in the outward act but in the heart — the one a stumbling of weakness, the other a defiance of will. They observe the solemn New Testament echo: “if we sin wilfully after we have received the knowledge of the truth, there remaineth no more sacrifice for sins” (Hebrews 10:26). They note that wilful, defiant sin shuts itself out of mercy. The verse thus condemns the presumptuous sinner — his guilt and doom explained in the next verse.
31:
On Numbers 15:31 — “Because he hath despised the word of the LORD, and hath broken his commandment, that soul shall utterly be cut off; his iniquity shall be upon him” — the witnesses mark the ground of the presumptuous sinner’s doom.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: because he hath despised the word of the LORD and broken his commandment, that soul shall utterly be cut off; his iniquity upon him. The Reformed Gill: he has broken the commandment through contempt of it, despising it as a command of God, paying no regard to it; otherwise such as sin ignorantly do not despise it.

The verse gives the reason. He despised God’s word and broke his commandment; his guilt is on himself. Contempt of God’s word brings the cutting-off.

So the witnesses see the ground of the presumptuous sinner’s utter doom: his contempt of the word of the LORD. The witnesses note the charge: “because he hath despised the word of the LORD, and hath broken his commandment” — Gill draws out the heart of the matter, that such a one has broken the commandment not in ignorance or weakness but through contempt of it, despising it as a command of God and paying no regard to it as his law; this is the very thing that distinguishes him from the sinner of ignorance, who reveres the command even while unwittingly breaking it. They mark the sentence: “that soul shall utterly be cut off” — the doubled phrase marks the certainty and severity of the excision; the despiser is wholly cut off from God’s people. They observe the weight of the closing words: “his iniquity shall be upon him” — the guilt of his sin rests on himself alone, not covered by any atonement, not borne away by any sacrifice; he must answer for it in his own person, the unpardoned weight of it upon his head. They note the solemn distinction the witnesses have pressed: that what shut the presumptuous sinner out of mercy was not the greatness of the act but the contempt of the heart; to despise God’s word is to refuse the very ground of forgiveness. They observe the gospel parallel, that to despise and reject the word and sacrifice of Christ leaves no other remedy, the sinner’s iniquity remaining on himself (Hebrews 10:29). They note that contempt of God’s word is its own undoing. The verse thus gives the ground of the doom — an instance of it following in the Sabbath-breaker.
32:
On Numbers 15:32 — “And while the children of Israel were in the wilderness, they found a man that gathered sticks upon the sabbath day” — the witnesses mark the Sabbath-breaker as an instance of presumptuous sin.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke: this was a case of the kind just supposed — the man despised the word of the LORD and broke his commandment (Numbers 15:31). Charles Ellicott: the incident is designed to illustrate the presumptuous sin. The Reformed Calvin: the Israelites were not always alike mad; we know not the exact year. Wesley: the law of the sabbath being plain and positive, this was a known and wilful sin.

The verse gives an instance. A man is found gathering sticks on the Sabbath. The high-handed sin is shown in a real case.

So the witnesses see, set immediately after the law of presumptuous sin, a living instance of it: the man who gathered sticks on the Sabbath. The witnesses note the connection the expositors draw: Clarke and Ellicott observe that this incident is placed here to illustrate the foregoing law of the high-handed sinner; the man “despised the word of the LORD and broke his commandment” (v. 31) in open defiance. They mark the nature of the offence: “a man that gathered sticks upon the sabbath day” — Wesley draws out that the law of the Sabbath was plain and positive, well known to all, so that this gathering of sticks (for kindling or labour) was no sin of ignorance but a known and wilful transgression, done in the face of a clear command (Exodus 31:14). They observe the note on the timing: Gill and Calvin record the Jewish opinion that, though the incident is recorded here, it may have happened earlier, in the first year; Calvin judges it best simply to follow the order of Moses’ narrative, and remarks that the Israelites were not always sunk in the same degree of madness, this being a particular case. They note the fitness of the example: the broken Sabbath, the sign of the covenant and the weekly confession of God as Creator and Redeemer, was a fit instance of the presumptuous sin that despises God’s word. They observe that the case put in the law was now met in fact, that the people might see the doom of high-handed sin enacted before them. They note that open defiance of a plain command is the very presumptuous sin. The verse thus gives the instance — the man brought to judgment in the next verses.
33:
On Numbers 15:33 — “And they that found him gathering sticks brought him unto Moses and Aaron, and unto all the congregation” — the witnesses mark the offender brought to judgment.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: they brought him unto Moses and Aaron and all the congregation. The Reformed Gill: they admonished him, as the Targum and Jarchi say, but he would not desist. Wesley: “to all the congregation” means to the rulers of the congregation.

The verse brings him in. Those who found him bring the offender to Moses, Aaron, and the congregation. The breach is laid before the authorities.

So the witnesses see the Sabbath-breaker brought before the leaders and the assembly for judgment. The witnesses note the action: “they that found him gathering sticks brought him unto Moses and Aaron” — those who discovered him in the act did not let it pass, but brought him before the appointed authorities; Gill records the tradition of the Targum and Jarchi that they first admonished him, warning him to desist, but he would not, persisting in his defiance — which, if so, deepened the presumption of his sin. They mark whom he was brought to: “Moses and Aaron, and unto all the congregation” — Wesley notes that “all the congregation” here means the rulers of the congregation, the elders who judged in such matters, before whom, with Moses and Aaron, the case was laid. They observe the orderliness the witnesses see: the offence was not punished by private hands or in haste, but brought before the lawful authority, that judgment might be done in due order and by warrant; even a plain breach of the Sabbath was to be tried, not avenged by the finder. They note that the bringing of him in showed the people’s sense of the gravity of the sin: an open profanation of the Sabbath, the sign of the covenant, was a matter for the whole congregation. They observe that the case was now before those appointed to judge, awaiting God’s direction. They note that order was kept even in dealing with sin. The verse thus brings him to judgment — his being put in ward following in the next verse.
34:
On Numbers 15:34 — “And they put him in ward, because it was not declared what should be done to him” — the witnesses mark the offender held until God should direct.

The Reformed Gill: they put him in ward, in a prison in the camp, perhaps the same in which the blasphemer was kept (Leviticus 24:12). Wesley: “they” are Moses, Aaron, and the seventy rulers; what should be done, that is, in what manner he should be put to death, which God here determines; the death itself was known.

The verse holds him in ward. He is kept in prison, for the manner of his death was not yet declared. They wait on God’s word.

So the witnesses see the offender kept in ward while the leaders awaited God’s direction as to his punishment. The witnesses note the holding: “they put him in ward” — Gill observes he was placed in a prison in the camp, perhaps the same in which the blasphemer had been kept until judgment (Leviticus 24:12); Wesley identifies the “they” as Moses, Aaron, and the seventy rulers. They mark the reason: “because it was not declared what should be done to him” — the witnesses are careful to define what was, and was not, yet known. Wesley draws out that it was not the death penalty itself that was in doubt, for the law had already declared that the Sabbath-breaker should be put to death (Exodus 31:14); rather, it was the manner of his death — by what kind of execution he should be cut off — that had not been determined, and which God now particularly appointed. They observe the reverence and caution the witnesses commend in this: the leaders did not presume to invent a punishment, nor to act before they had God’s word, but held the man in custody until the LORD should direct; they would do nothing in so grave a matter without express warrant. They note that this waiting on God, even when the general law was clear, shows the care of Moses and the rulers to follow God exactly. They observe that the case was referred to God, who alone could appoint the manner of judgment. They note that patience and inquiry preceded execution. The verse thus holds him in ward — the LORD’s sentence following in the next verse.
35:
On Numbers 15:35 — “And the LORD said unto Moses, The man shall be surely put to death: all the congregation shall stone him with stones without the camp” — the witnesses mark God’s sentence on the Sabbath-breaker.

The Reformed Gill: Moses consulted the LORD in the tabernacle, at the most holy place, from above the mercy seat, where God promised to commune with him.

The verse gives the sentence. God decrees the man be stoned by all the congregation outside the camp. The manner of death is appointed.

So the witnesses see God himself pronounce the sentence on the Sabbath-breaker, appointing the manner of his death. The witnesses note the source of the word: Gill observes that Moses consulted the LORD upon this affair in the tabernacle, at the most holy place, from above the mercy seat, where God had promised to meet and commune with him (Exodus 25:22); the judgment came not from Moses but from God. They mark the sentence: “the man shall be surely put to death” — the death penalty already threatened in the law was here confirmed and ordered to be executed; the wilful profanation of the Sabbath was a capital crime. They observe the manner appointed: “all the congregation shall stone him with stones without the camp” — the man was to be stoned, and that by the whole congregation, and outside the camp; the public execution by the people’s own hands marked the public guilt and the people’s share in purging it, and the putting-out beyond the camp marked the casting of the offender from the holy community. They note the gravity the witnesses see in so severe a sentence for what might seem a small act: it was not the gathering of a few sticks in itself, but the open contempt of God’s plain command and the profanation of the covenant sign, that drew so heavy a doom; the presumptuous sin was judged according to its real heinousness. They observe the typology some draw: the offender stoned without the camp recalls that Christ, made sin for us, suffered without the gate (Hebrews 13:12). They note that God’s holiness will not be despised. The verse thus gives the sentence — its execution following in the next verse.
36:
On Numbers 15:36 — “And all the congregation brought him without the camp, and stoned him with stones, and he died; as the LORD commanded Moses” — the witnesses mark the sentence executed in obedience.

The grammatical-historical Adam Clarke refers to the stoning of Leviticus 24:23. The Reformed Gill: what was done by order of Moses and the elders is said to be done by the whole congregation, though by a few persons the man was actually brought out and stoned.

The verse records the execution. The congregation brings him out and stones him, as the LORD commanded. The sentence is carried out in obedience.

So the witnesses see the sentence carried out exactly as God had commanded, the Sabbath-breaker stoned and slain. The witnesses note the obedience: “all the congregation brought him without the camp, and stoned him with stones, and he died; as the LORD commanded Moses” — the people did precisely what God had appointed, in the manner and place he had named; the repeated “as the LORD commanded” marks their exact compliance. They mark Gill’s note on the phrase “all the congregation”: what was done by the order of Moses and the elders is ascribed to the whole congregation, though in fact the man was brought out and stoned by a few persons acting on behalf of all; the act was the people’s, done in their name and by their authority. They observe the place once more: “without the camp” — the execution outside the holy encampment, putting the offender from the community he had defiled. They note the solemnity the witnesses see: the people obeyed a hard command, putting to death one of their own for the profanation of the Sabbath, that the holiness of God’s ordinance and the gravity of presumptuous sin might be vindicated before all Israel. They observe that the law of the high-handed sinner (vv. 30–31) was thus shown in act: the despiser of God’s word was cut off, his iniquity upon him, no atonement intervening. They note that obedience, even in severity, honoured God. They observe the typology held from the foregoing verse, of suffering without the gate. The verse thus records the execution in obedience — the law turning, in mercy, to the fringes of remembrance.
37:
On Numbers 15:37 — “And the LORD spake unto Moses, saying” — the witnesses mark the new word introducing the fringes.

The Reformed Gill: spoken after the foregoing laws and the order for stoning the Sabbath-breaker; what follows is fitly connected, to put them in mind of the commandments. The Puritan Henry: provision had been made for the pardon of sins of ignorance; now an expedient is provided for the preventing of such sins.
