The verse opens a new precept. God speaks again to give the law of the fringes. A means to prevent sin is now provided.

So the witnesses see a fresh divine word open the law of the fringes, given to help the people remember and keep God’s commandments. The witnesses note the fitting connection: Gill observes that this word came after the foregoing laws and the order for stoning the Sabbath-breaker, and that what follows is aptly joined to them, being designed to put the people in mind of God’s commandments, that they might not forget and so transgress. They mark Henry’s discernment of the purpose: provision had just been made, in the sin offerings, for the pardon of sins of ignorance; now God provides an expedient for the preventing of such sins — the fringes, a constant reminder of the commandments, that the people might not fall into the very errors for which atonement had been appointed. They observe the order of grace the witnesses see: God first provides the remedy for sin (the atonement), and then the means to keep from sin (the reminder); his care reaches both to the pardon of past failings and the prevention of future ones. They note the fitness of giving such a help after the Sabbath-breaker’s doom: that the people, warned by the severity against presumptuous sin, might be furnished with a daily aid to obedience, lest they too forget and stray. They observe that the new word introduces not a burden but a help, a token to keep God’s law in mind. They note that God is as careful to prevent sin as to pardon it. The verse thus opens the law of the fringes — the fringes themselves commanded in the next verse.
38:
On Numbers 15:38 — “Bid them that they make them fringes in the borders of their garments… and that they put upon the fringe of the borders a ribband of blue” — the witnesses mark the fringes and the blue ribband.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: fringes in the borders of their garments, with a ribband of blue. Adam Clarke: the fringes were emblematical of the commands of God (Numbers 15:39). Charles Ellicott: better, tassels on the corners, with a thread of blue. The Reformed Gill: bound on all Israelites, not Gentiles. Wesley: threads standing out, in the four borders (Deuteronomy 22:12).

The verse commands the fringes. Tassels on the garment’s corners, with a ribband of blue. A visible token is appointed.

So the witnesses see the fringes commanded, tassels on the borders of the garment, marked with a thread of blue. The witnesses note the thing appointed: “fringes in the borders of their garments… a ribband of blue” — Wesley and Ellicott describe them as tassels or threads standing out on the corners of the outer garment, on the four borders or quarters (Deuteronomy 22:12), each tassel bound or marked with a cord of blue. They mark whom the law bound: Gill notes that all Israelites — priests, Levites, common Israelites, proselytes, and freed servants — were to wear the fringes, but not the Gentiles; the token belonged to the covenant people. They observe the meaning Clarke draws out, from the next verse: the fringes were emblematical of the commandments of God; though there is no natural likeness between a tassel and a precept, yet, being divinely appointed for this use, they served as a constant sign to call the commandments to mind. They note the significance of the blue, which the witnesses and tradition observe: the colour of the heavens, fitly marking that the commandments were from above, and drawing the thoughts upward to God who gave them. They observe the abuse the New Testament records, that the Pharisees enlarged the borders of their garments for show and pride (Matthew 23:5), perverting a token of obedience into a badge of vanity; the witnesses warn against resting in the outward sign. They note that the fringe was meant not for display but for remembrance. The verse thus commands the fringes and the blue — their purpose declared in the next verse.
39:
On Numbers 15:39 — “And it shall be unto you for a fringe, that ye may look upon it, and remember all the commandments of the LORD… and that ye seek not after your own heart and your own eyes” — the witnesses mark the purpose of the fringes.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott: “that ye seek not after your own heart,” or go not about after it. The Reformed Calvin: God had no other object but to exercise the Jews in constant meditation upon the law. Wesley: the blue ribband renders the fringe more visible by its distinct colour.

The verse gives the purpose. They look on the fringe and remember the commandments, not following their own heart and eyes. The token guards against wandering.

So the witnesses see the purpose of the fringes declared: to keep the commandments of God ever in remembrance, and to guard against the wandering of heart and eye. The witnesses note the looking and remembering: “that ye may look upon it, and remember all the commandments of the LORD, and do them” — the fringe was a thing to be seen, that the sight of it might call to mind the commandments, and the remembrance lead to the doing; the eye was to teach the heart, and the heart to move the hand. They mark Calvin’s plain statement of the design: God had no other object in this precept but to exercise the Jews in constant meditation upon the law, that it might never slip from their thoughts. They observe the warning against self-will: “that ye seek not after your own heart and your own eyes, after which ye use to go a whoring” — Ellicott would render it “go not about after your own heart”; the fringe was to check the people from following the wandering desires of their own hearts and the lusts of their own eyes, which led them astray from God into spiritual whoredom and idolatry. They note the double use the witnesses see: the fringe both drew the mind toward God’s law and drew it back from its own corrupt inclinations; remembrance of the command was the guard against the wandering of desire. They observe the lesson for all: that the heart, left to itself, strays, and needs the constant reminder of God’s word to keep it in the way. They note that Wesley marks the blue thread as making the fringe the more visible, the readier to catch the eye. The verse thus gives the purpose of the fringes — the end of holiness following in the next verse.
40:
On Numbers 15:40 — “That ye may remember, and do all my commandments, and be holy unto your God” — the witnesses mark the end of the fringes in holiness.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch: that ye may remember and do all my commandments, and be holy unto your God. The Reformed Gill: repeated, that the end and use of the fringes be particularly noted, that they not rest in the ceremony. Wesley: “be ye holy” — purged from sin and sincerely devoted to God.

The verse names the end. They remember, do the commandments, and are holy to God. The token aims at obedience and holiness.

So the witnesses see the whole end of the fringes gathered into one: remembrance, obedience, and holiness. The witnesses note the chain: “that ye may remember, and do all my commandments, and be holy unto your God” — the looking led to remembering, the remembering to doing, and the doing to holiness; the fringe was no end in itself, but a means to the keeping of the commandments and the sanctifying of the people. They mark Gill’s observation that the purpose is here repeated, that the end and use of the fringes might be particularly noted and laid to heart, lest the people rest in the outward ceremony of wearing them and neglect the obedience they were meant to serve; the sign was nothing without the substance. They observe the goal declared: “be holy unto your God” — Wesley draws out that to be holy is to be purged from sin and sincerely devoted to God; the fringes aimed at this, that Israel, remembering and doing the commandments, might be set apart and consecrated to the LORD. They note the order the witnesses see: holiness is reached through obedience, and obedience through remembrance, and remembrance is helped by the appointed token; God provides the outward aid to serve the inward end. They observe the warning implicit throughout: that the mere wearing of the fringe, without the remembering and doing, profits nothing; the Pharisees’ enlarged borders, worn without obedience, were vanity. They note that the aim of every ordinance is holiness. The verse thus names the end of the fringes — grounded, in the last verse, in the redemption from Egypt.
41:
On Numbers 15:41 — “I am the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt, to be your God: I am the LORD your God” — the witnesses mark the redemption from Egypt as the ground of obedience.

The Reformed Gill: their Creator, Preserver, and Benefactor, their Lord and covenant God, who brought them out of Egypt to be their God. Calvin: he confirms the command to be holy by a reason — that God redeemed them for this end, that he might be their God. Wesley: though justly displeased, and keeping them forty years in the wilderness, yet he will not utterly cast them off, but continue their God.

The verse grounds all in redemption. The LORD their God brought them out of Egypt to be their God. The deliverance is the reason for obedience and holiness.

So the witnesses see the whole chapter sealed with the ground of all obedience: the LORD’s redemption of Israel from Egypt to be their God. The witnesses note the solemn self-naming: “I am the LORD your God, which brought you out of the land of Egypt” — Gill marks him as their Creator, Preserver, and Benefactor, their Lord and Sovereign, their covenant God and Father, who had redeemed them from bondage. They mark the end of the redemption: “to be your God” — Calvin draws out that God here confirms the command to be holy (v. 40) by this reason, that he redeemed them for this very purpose, that he might be their God and be solemnly honoured by them; the deliverance laid them under the obligation of obedience and holiness. They observe the comfort Wesley finds in the renewed claim, set at the close of a chapter so full of judgment: though God was justly displeased with Israel for their rebellion, and would keep them forty years in the wilderness, yet he will not utterly cast them off, but continues to be their God; the covenant holds. They note the fitness of grounding the fringes, and all the commandments, in the redemption from Egypt: because the LORD had bought them out of bondage to be his own, they owed him remembrance, obedience, and holiness; grace is the spring of duty. They observe the doubled “I am the LORD your God,” opening and closing the verse, that the truth be pressed home and the people rest in it. They note that redemption is the foundation of all the law. The verse thus grounds all in redemption — the close of the chapter and of these laws.
